note: Derived viral teardowns of real breakout videos (over-reached the creator's following). Our analysis in our words; no verbatim transcript or source URL. The full audit version stays local.

teardowns.openingVisual: A dark-skinned creator in a grey headwrap and pink zip-top, head tilted up, holding a makeup brush loaded with near-black product to her cheek; a small inset box in the lower-left shows the original lighter-skinned creator she is responding to.
teardowns.lighting: Soft, even key light against a clean light-grey/cream room; flattering and bright, not low-fi.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Tight selfie close-up, face fills the frame, with a stitch/inset window pinning the original creator she is clapping back at.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Minimal, deliberately understated — headwrap, gold hoops, dewy skin — so all attention is on the brush and the deadpan delivery.
teardowns.hookTechnique: She literally paints the 'darkest shade' foundation on as 'black face paint,' turning a shade-range complaint into a visual that you cannot scroll past because you need to know whether she is serious.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The duet structure plants an unresolved conflict — you keep watching to see how far she takes the bit and how she lands the comeback at the original creator.
teardowns.viralMechanism: It rides an active discourse (foundation shade inclusivity, @golloria) and weaponizes deadpan satire; viewers share to take a side, and the caption 'you all way too dramatic' is itself a quote-able stance that drives the comment war.

dimensionRatings.hook: very-strong — paints provocation onto her face
dimensionRatings.format: strong — duet pins the conflict instantly
dimensionRatings.visual: strong — bright clean shot, brush draws eye
dimensionRatings.audio: strong — flat sarcastic delivery sells satire

teardowns.stealThis: Don't argue a complaint — physically perform the exaggerated version of it on camera so the rebuttal is a visual the feed can't ignore.

teardowns.openingVisual: A woman in glasses filling the whole frame, face caked in still-unblended powder and bright green eyeshadow with a flushed under-eye, looking dead into the lens; large yellow caption 'makeup touch up' sits over her chin.
teardowns.lighting: Harsh indoor overhead/office light, unflattering on purpose — it exposes the uneven powder and is part of the joke.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Extreme handheld selfie close-up, slightly low angle, so the in-progress face is unavoidable.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Anti-glam: visible flyaways, mid-process makeup, ordinary clothes — the gap between 'tutorial' framing and the chaotic result is the entertainment.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Frames itself as a normal 'touch up' tutorial while showing a face that looks anything but finished, so the viewer stays to see whether it's serious, satire, or a trainwreck.
teardowns.retentionDevice: An ongoing 'is she actually going to fix this?' open loop, plus the 'Dad, is that you?' interruption mid-video that resets attention.
teardowns.viralMechanism: It works as forced-productivity comedy: the value is watching someone perform a beauty ritual with zero polish and full confidence, which reads as funny-relatable and gets stitched/dueted; the absurd length (288s) survives because each segment adds a new bit.

dimensionRatings.visual: strong — unflattering light is the joke
dimensionRatings.audio: strong — casual narration plus dad cameo

teardowns.stealThis: Use a familiar tutorial frame ('touch up') but let the visible reality contradict it — the gap between promise and image is the hook.

teardowns.openingVisual: A young creator in a satin leopard-print bonnet and black hoodie, face flushed bright pink (a skincare/mask stage), staring intensely into the camera in a cluttered home setting; pink script text reads 'grwm fo[r]'.
teardowns.onScreenTextHook: grwm for the first day of school!!!
teardowns.lighting: Flat indoor home light with a pink color cast on the skin; low-fi and unpolished.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Handheld selfie, head-and-shoulders, slightly chaotic framing matching the manic energy.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Bonnet + hoodie 'just woke up' look; the styling signals authenticity and the relatable not-ready-for-school chaos.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Opens mid-rant with a confrontational direct address ('what the f*** are you doing') that pattern-interrupts a calm beauty feed and dares you to keep watching.
teardowns.retentionDevice: Repeated escalating 'get ready with me for the third week / for school' beats create a chant-like loop, and the aggressive tone keeps tension high across the short 10s.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Shock-comedy in a beauty wrapper: the mismatch between sweet GRWM expectations and a profane us-vs-them tirade is inherently shareable to friends ('this is so unhinged'), driving the 10.5% engagement rate on a tiny account.

dimensionRatings.visual: weak — pink masked face but cluttered messy frame
dimensionRatings.audio: very-strong — manic profane delivery is the draw

teardowns.stealThis: Open on raw confrontational energy, not a greeting — an unhinged direct address out-performs a polite 'hey guys' for scroll-stop.

teardowns.openingVisual: A teen creator with pink-streaked dark hair and bold brows in a pale tank top, mid-sentence in a messy bedroom; centered white text 'GRWM for school' and a TikTok search-bar overlay 'GRWM for school' frame it as part of a trending search.
teardowns.lighting: Natural daylight from a window, slightly blown out — ordinary teen-bedroom lighting.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Selfie at arm's length, head-and-shoulders, casual handheld bob.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Everyday tank top, undone hair, no studio polish — the relatable 'real teenager' aesthetic that the school-GRWM trend rewards.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Pins the video to a high-volume seasonal search ('GRWM for school') so it surfaces to an already-activated audience, then opens on a confident first-day-back premise.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The promise of a 'final look' reveal at the end (plus relatable self-deprecating asides like losing the beauty blender) keeps viewers to the payoff.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Seasonal trend-surfing: back-to-school GRWM is a recurring high-demand format, and a believable, imperfect teen doing the routine collects mass relatability and saves/shares from the same-age audience.

dimensionRatings.hook: strong — rides a seasonal search trend
dimensionRatings.visual: weak — flat bedroom light, low polish

teardowns.stealThis: Mirror the exact trending search phrase as on-screen text so the algorithm files you under an active query the audience is already scrolling.

teardowns.openingVisual: A heavily muscled older man in a white tank doing a standing cable exercise in a green-accented commercial gym, shot from the side; white caption 'Most people don't even know this muscle exists.' sits across his torso.
teardowns.onScreenTextHook: Most people don't even know this muscle exists.
teardowns.lighting: Bright fluorescent gym lighting, clean and visible; ordinary commercial-gym look.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Mid-distance side profile that shows the full muscle contraction, then cuts closer on the working area.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Old-school bodybuilder presentation — tank, conspicuous physique — which lends the 'secret muscle' claim borrowed authority.
teardowns.hookTechnique: A curiosity-gap belief-challenge ('a muscle you don't know exists, that's why you don't have it') that makes the viewer feel they're missing something everyone else is too.
teardowns.retentionDevice: Withholds exactly which muscle and how to train it, forcing the viewer to stay for the 'train it like it matters' payoff.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Combines an authority physique with a contrarian secret-knowledge frame; gym audiences save and share 'hidden muscle' tips, though the modest 2% engagement signals it spread more on reach than deep resonance.

dimensionRatings.format: strong — physique proof under the claim
dimensionRatings.visual: strong — clean gym demo shows the movement

teardowns.stealThis: Frame a basic tip as hidden knowledge 'most people don't even know' — the implied gap in the viewer's own training is the hook.

teardowns.openingVisual: A clip of a woman straining and screaming on a chest-press machine (a stitched original) cuts to the creator in a dark hoodie, bare-faced in a dim room, holding up four fingers as he starts his take; on-screen text 'I try my best to not scream.'
teardowns.onScreenTextHook: I try my best to not scream
teardowns.lighting: Dim, warm indoor home lighting on the reaction half; the stitched gym clip is brighter and grittier.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Reaction selfie close-up intercut with the full-body gym source clip.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Plain hoodie, no styling — positions him as a credible everyman correcting a viral clip.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Stitches an already-viral 'gym screaming lady' clip, borrowing its reach, then opens a contrarian correction that invites the viewer to take a side.
teardowns.retentionDevice: Sets up a 'here's why that's actually wrong' explainer loop, so viewers stay to get the physiology verdict and to judge whether he's right.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Reaction-to-a-controversy: piggybacking a trending clip plus a confident myth-bust generates comment debate (8.9% engagement) and shares from both people who agree and people defending the original.

dimensionRatings.hook: strong — borrows a viral clip's momentum

teardowns.stealThis: Stitch a clip that's already moving and add a confident contrarian correction — you inherit its reach and farm the debate.

teardowns.openingVisual: A shirtless young man with curly hair stands in a plain home hallway facing the camera, soft body visible; he holds up a hand mid-gesture — the unglamorous 'before/current' self shown without flattering angles.
teardowns.lighting: Flat natural home lighting, no contouring shadows — honest and exposed, which is the point.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Static head-to-stomach shot at conversational distance so the body is fully visible.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Deliberately undressed and unstyled to make the vulnerability and the eventual progress real.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Visual vulnerability — showing his real, unflattering body — pattern-interrupts a feed full of shredded physiques and earns immediate empathy.
teardowns.retentionDevice: An emotional motivational voiceover ('if you feel like you're not enough') builds toward a reassurance payoff, and the caption '10 months in' promises an implied transformation arc.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Identity-and-encouragement: viewers who feel insecure see themselves in him, and the affirming message gets saved (58k saves) and shared as motivation; the 11% engagement reflects genuine emotional resonance, not just reach.

dimensionRatings.hook: strong — raw body vulnerability stops scroll
dimensionRatings.visual: strong — honest unflattering framing builds trust

teardowns.stealThis: Lead with the unflattering, honest 'before' on camera — vulnerability earns the empathy and saves that a polished physique can't.

teardowns.videoFormat: moody gym b-roll over meme audio
teardowns.openingVisual: A silhouetted lifter using a seated machine against a bright window, shot dark and cinematic in a concrete gym; white subtitle 'Impresiones a tu propio padre' (then 'mi padre está muerto señor') overlays a viral comedic dialogue audio.
teardowns.lighting: High-contrast backlit silhouette against window glare — moody, aspirational gym-cinema look.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Wide static side shot keeping the lifter small and shadowed, prioritizing mood over detail.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Anonymous silhouette, hoodie, headphones — universal 'grind' avatar the viewer can project onto.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Pairs aspirational training imagery with a recognizable comedic meme-audio (the 'impress your father' bit), so the recognition of the sound is the hook, not the visual.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The escalating absurd dialogue ('my father is dead, sir... abandoned us twice') is a known audio loop people stay to hear pay off and re-watch.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Trend-audio adaptation: dropping a moody gym aesthetic onto a viral sound lets fitness creators ride the audio's reach; the very low share rate vs high saves suggests it traveled as motivational save-bait riding the sound.

dimensionRatings.hook: strong — recognizable meme audio hooks instantly
dimensionRatings.audio: very-strong — the viral sound is the engine

teardowns.stealThis: Lay your niche's most aspirational b-roll over a currently-trending audio — the sound does the reach, your visuals do the vibe.

teardowns.openingVisual: A rendered cross-section of an artery clogged with yellow plaque, red blood cells squeezing past, with a white supplement capsule entering frame against a faint anatomical-diagram background; yellow caption 'If you take this'.
teardowns.lighting: Rendered CGI lighting — glossy, high-saturation medical animation, no real-world set.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Macro CGI push-in on the artery and pill; no human, no creator presence.
teardowns.creatorStyling: None — this is a faceless animated direct-response ad, not a creator video.
teardowns.hookTechnique: An outsized physiological promise ('arteries withered to a straw forcibly dilated to twice their width in 3 days') with a fear-based symptom callout grabs an older health-anxious viewer.
teardowns.retentionDevice: Escalating dramatic stakes (gasping for air, heart's desperate struggle) plus a money-back guarantee keep the pitch moving to the CTA.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Classic fear-then-mechanism VSL: it scares, names a 'mechanism' (nitric oxide power-washer), and de-risks with a refund. The 0.75% engagement rate (likes 9k on 1.9M views) is a red flag the reach is paid/boosted, not organic resonance.

dimensionRatings.visual: strong — vivid artery CGI grabs attention

teardowns.stealThis: Only the structure is steal-worthy (fear → named mechanism → guarantee); treat the reach as bought, not proof the creative resonates.

teardowns.openingVisual: A young Black woman with a sleek bob in a black top sits in a dim bedroom (purple headphones visible behind her) speaking directly to camera, conversational and grounded — no product yet in frame.
teardowns.lighting: Soft warm bedroom lamp light; intimate, low-fi, trustworthy.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Selfie close-up, face-centered, steady — a confidante telling you something.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Understated everyday look that keeps the focus on the spoken story and credibility.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Borrowed authority via storytime — 'I used to work for this doctor who stressed vitamin D for Black women' — anchors the recommendation in a credible third party.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The narrative ('why did he care so much?') opens a small mystery, then resolves into a community-specific health reason that the target audience needs to hear out.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Identity + authority: it speaks directly to Black women's vitamin D deficiency with a doctor's backing, which the community saves (47k) and shares as a trusted PSA; the huge 21% engagement reflects strong in-group resonance.

dimensionRatings.visual: weak — dim plain bedroom, low detail

teardowns.stealThis: Anchor a health rec in a specific credible third party and a specific identity group — borrowed authority plus belonging drives saves.

teardowns.openingVisual: A blonde woman in a grey cardigan with a stethoscope around her neck sits at a wooden table behind an empty clear plastic cup, mid-sentence; she then pours water from a bottle over green capsules in the cup to demo dissolution.
teardowns.lighting: Clean even indoor light against a calm grey wall with a plant — composed, credible 'clinical' set.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Static medium shot framing the table-top demo so the cup and pills are visible alongside her face.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Stethoscope and cardigan signal medical authority; the prop borrows clinician credibility for the magnesium claims.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Symptom-mirroring open ('if you take your bra off and everything falls, you need magnesium citrate') makes each viewer self-diagnose, hooking them into their own line of the list.
teardowns.retentionDevice: Rapid-fire 'if you [symptom], you need [type]' stacking keeps viewers scanning for their own symptom, and the closing 'buying these separately costs a fortune' sets up the bundle CTA.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Diagnostic listicle: personalized symptom callouts maximize saves (91k) as a reference, and the relatable body complaints get shared; modest 2.1% engagement on 14.2M views shows broad reach with light per-view resonance.

dimensionRatings.visual: strong — stethoscope + dissolve demo adds proof

teardowns.stealThis: Build a symptom→solution listicle where each line makes a different viewer point at themselves; that's what drives the save-for-later behavior.

teardowns.openingVisual: A composed middle-aged man in a navy quarter-zip stands outside a 'Boots' pharmacy storefront, gesturing to camera; bold white text 'Korean SECRET to GLASS skin at 50 in Boots' with a smaller 'Listen carefully' below.
teardowns.onScreenTextHook: Korean SECRET to GLASS skin at 50 in Boots
teardowns.lighting: Bright outdoor daylight, clean and credible; the recognizable storefront grounds it.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Selfie walk-up shot framed against the named retailer so the location reads as legit.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Mature, put-together 'trustworthy uncle' presentation that backs the at-50 glass-skin promise.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Stacks a curiosity-gap secret + an age qualifier + a specific retailer ('at 50, in Boots') so an older skincare buyer feels it's made for exactly them.
teardowns.retentionDevice: 'Listen carefully' plus a numbered countdown of three products withholds the full set until the end, pulling viewers through the list.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Aspirational age-defying listicle anchored to a real shop: the specificity (Korean, glass skin, 50, Boots) makes it feel actionable, and the high share count signals it traveled as a 'where do I buy this' recommendation.
teardowns.stealThis: Pin a curiosity-secret to a concrete, recognizable place and a specific age — specificity converts a vague tip into a 'where do I get it' share.

teardowns.openingVisual: A blonde woman, bare-faced with visible texture/redness, fills the lower frame against a plain ceiling; large centered white text 'I'm confident that us girlies are being scammed by the skincare industry.'
teardowns.onScreenTextHook: I'm confident that us girlies are being scammed by the skincare industry
teardowns.lighting: Flat natural indoor light that intentionally shows her real skin — supports the honesty frame.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Low selfie angle looking up, face in bottom third, text occupying the top — designed for the text to be read first.
teardowns.creatorStyling: No makeup, no styling — the bare skin is the credential for an anti-industry claim.
teardowns.hookTechnique: A confident contrarian accusation against an entire industry ('we're being scammed') provokes both agreement and defense, demanding a reaction.
teardowns.retentionDevice: A personal-story open loop (her boyfriend with perfect skin using just two cheap products) keeps viewers for the reveal of what those products are.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Industry-contrarian storytime: the 'expensive routines are a scam, here's the cheap truth' arc earns trust and 10.4% engagement, with shares from people who feel validated; bare-faced honesty is the proof device.

dimensionRatings.visual: strong — bare skin authenticates the claim

teardowns.stealThis: Accuse the whole category of scamming people, then withhold the cheap alternative — the open loop plus your bare-faced proof carries it.

teardowns.openingVisual: A screen-recorded TikTok LIVE: a young woman touches her nose mid skincare routine, live-chat comments and floating heart reactions overlaid, plus a 'Daily Ranking' gift bar and the title text 'Amanda putting us on her skin care routine.'
teardowns.onScreenTextHook: Amanda putting us on her skin care routine
teardowns.lighting: Casual bedroom light captured through a LIVE stream — soft, slightly compressed, very low-fi.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Vertical screen-capture of a LIVE, so chat UI and gift animations frame the creator's face.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Glam-casual influencer look, but the raw LIVE-capture UI is what signals 'unfiltered, happening now.'
teardowns.hookTechnique: Reposting a LIVE moment with chat visible borrows the parasocial 'you're in the room' energy and the implied credibility of a real-time crowd asking questions.
teardowns.retentionDevice: Live chat questions ('how many syringes did you get') create voyeuristic micro-stories, and the casual product callout (Medicube pore pads) lands as an offhand real recommendation.
teardowns.viralMechanism: LIVE-clip repurposing: clipping a candid stream feels more authentic than a produced video, and the casual TikTok-Shop product mention gets saved as a real-person endorsement.
teardowns.stealThis: Clip a candid LIVE with the chat still visible — the real-time crowd and unpolished UI make a product mention read as genuine, not an ad.

teardowns.openingVisual: The creator in a white top stands lower-left while a large washed-out projection of another woman in a bonnet doing a face-taping/strap routine fills the screen behind her; she points up at it, Spanish caption 'Esta es la exa[geración]'.
teardowns.lighting: Clean light on the foreground creator; the reacted clip is intentionally blown-out/desaturated to mark it as 'the thing being judged.'
teardowns.cameraFraming: Green-screen/duet composite — small reactor in front, large source clip behind — so the contrast of stances is instant.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Minimal, neutral foreground styling positioning her as the sensible voice against the over-the-top routine.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Reacts to an absurd-looking 10-step nightly routine (chin strap, masks, multiple serums) — the visual excess of the source clip is the scroll-stopper.
teardowns.retentionDevice: A running 'let's analyze this' critique with escalating 'why does she even need all this' beats keeps viewers for the verdict ('less is more').
teardowns.viralMechanism: Contrarian critique of viral excess: judging an over-engineered routine lets the audience feel sane and validated, generating debate and shares; the visible absurdity of the source carries the reach.

dimensionRatings.hook: strong — reacts to visually absurd routine
dimensionRatings.visual: strong — exaggerated source clip is the draw

teardowns.stealThis: React to the most visually excessive example in your niche from a sensible-voice angle — the absurd clip is the hook, your verdict is the share.

teardowns.openingVisual: An overhead shot of bright green matcha being poured from a pale-green jug straight over a bowl of brown chocolate cereal, an ice cube tumbling in; bold white text 'is this illegal???' sits over the bowl.
teardowns.lighting: Clean overhead kitchen light on a marble counter; high color contrast (vivid green on brown) is engineered for the thumbnail.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Top-down close-up on the bowl so the unnatural food combination fills the frame.
teardowns.creatorStyling: No creator on camera — hands and product only, all attention on the gross/curious act.
teardowns.hookTechnique: A transgressive food act ('pouring matcha on cereal — is this illegal?') triggers disgust-curiosity; you watch to judge whether it's genius or cursed.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The satisfying/horrifying pour itself, plus the open question in the text, makes viewers stay to react and comment their verdict.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Shock-prop + tribe-bait: it fuses the matcha-obsession trend with a polarizing 'is this allowed' food act; the absurd 13.7% engagement and huge share count come from tag-your-friend disgust/delight, not from selling anything.

dimensionRatings.format: strong — overhead ASMR pour is hypnotic
dimensionRatings.audio: strong — ASMR pour sound carries it

teardowns.stealThis: Stage one polarizing visual act and label it with a dare ('is this illegal?') — disgust-curiosity drives shares no product pitch can match.

teardowns.openingVisual: A woman lies in a hospital recliner under a fuzzy cow-print blanket, eyes closed with an IV pole and monitors behind her in a real infusion ward; white text 'RIP to my Matcha era' over the scene, set to the 'Rasputin' meme audio.
teardowns.lighting: Real fluorescent hospital lighting — the authentic clinical setting is the joke's anchor.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Handheld POV looking down at herself in the hospital chair, then cuts; framing sells 'this really happened.'
teardowns.creatorStyling: Patient-in-treatment realism (blanket, IV) deliberately undercut by the comedic caption and meme sound.
teardowns.hookTechnique: Pairs a genuinely alarming visual (hospital IV) with a trivial cause ('matcha blocks iron absorption') and the 'you're probably wondering how I ended up here' meme frame, creating instant comedic dissonance.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The 'how I ended up in this situation' meme structure plus the Rasputin audio is a known loop people stay to hear resolve.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Meme-format self-deprecation: over-dramatizing a tiny health fact via a recognizable audio makes it endlessly relatable to matcha lovers, driving 222k shares as a 'this is literally me' forward.

dimensionRatings.visual: strong — real hospital setting sells the bit
dimensionRatings.audio: very-strong — Rasputin meme audio is the engine

teardowns.stealThis: Dramatize a trivial niche fact with a real high-stakes setting and a known meme audio — the dissonance is the whole joke.

teardowns.openingVisual: A man with locs sits in a car, mouth open in a disgusted/confused reaction, holding up a clear cup of bright green iced matcha toward the lens; white text 'POV: Drinking Matcha for the first time' across the top.
teardowns.onScreenTextHook: POV: Drinking Matcha for the first time
teardowns.lighting: Natural daylight through the car windows — casual, unstaged, very relatable.
teardowns.cameraFraming: Selfie close-up in the car with the green drink thrust toward camera, so his expression and the product share the frame.
teardowns.creatorStyling: Completely casual — just a guy in his car — which makes the honest reaction believable.
teardowns.hookTechnique: A first-time-trying-it POV plus an instantly funny visceral reaction ('tastes like a vanilla football field... this is grass') hooks both matcha fans and skeptics.
teardowns.retentionDevice: The escalating string of absurd taste comparisons keeps viewers laughing to the final 'yep'; the open question of whether he'll like it holds attention.
teardowns.viralMechanism: Reaction-bait on a trend's polarizing taste: matcha lovers defend it and haters agree, fueling a 16.8% engagement rate and 581k shares as a comment-war and tag-a-friend video.

dimensionRatings.format: strong — POV reaction is endlessly relatable
dimensionRatings.visual: strong — vivid green drink + expressive face

teardowns.stealThis: Film an honest first-time reaction to a polarizing trendy item with quotable similes — defenders and haters both share it to argue.
